Cell-type analysis

RCN Analysis

Mixed

Epi dominant

Stroma dominant

Mixed

Speaker notes:
RCN5 (Stromal core) dominates at 1.1M cells — the connective tissue background
RCN3 (Dysplastic core) at 70% dysplastic epithelial is the cleanest tumour niche
RCN4 (Normal epithelial) at 62% normal is well-separated from RCN3
RCN1 (B cell-rich) is the most distinctive immune niche — 43% B cells plus 12% CD8+, very unusual composition, but small (47k cells)
RCN7 (Immune-stromal-dysplastic interface) is the most mixed — this is the invasion front/tumour edge
RCN8 (Myeloid-T cell inflamed) has 15% M2, 15% CD8+, 12% CD4+ — a classically inflamed niche with high M2 content suggesting possible immune suppression
RCN6 (Stromal core 2) only 82% self-adjacent vs RCN5's 95% — clearly a transitional zone, not a pure stromal niche. The 12% spillover to RCN5 confirms it

RCN spatial maps

Speaker notes:
RCN5 (Stromal core) dominates at 1.1M cells — the connective tissue background
RCN3 (Dysplastic core) at 70% dysplastic epithelial is the cleanest tumour niche
RCN4 (Normal epithelial) at 62% normal is well-separated from RCN3
RCN1 (B cell-rich) is the most distinctive immune niche — 43% B cells plus 12% CD8+, very unusual composition, but small (47k cells)
RCN7 (Immune-stromal-dysplastic interface) is the most mixed — this is the invasion front/tumour edge
RCN8 (Myeloid-T cell inflamed) has 15% M2, 15% CD8+, 12% CD4+ — a classically inflamed niche with high M2 content suggesting possible immune suppression
RCN6 (Stromal core 2) only 82% self-adjacent vs RCN5's 95% — clearly a transitional zone, not a pure stromal niche. The 12% spillover to RCN5 confirms it

Results from SPACEstat - NN distance

Speaker notes:
This checks how these cell types sit and if they are relatively close to each other. Nothing significant for Nearest Neighbour Distance as nothing survived FDR correction. But we see tregs are not very clustered in quick regs.

Results from SPACEstat - Cluster density

Results from SPACEstat - Gini Density

Speaker notes:
This one is the most interesting, quick-reg has lower Gini which means more uniform distribution for most immune cell type. The "non-reg vs rest" direction is consistent show non-reg has higher Gini (patchier).

Results from SPACEstat - Lesion-stromal-Interface 50um

Speaker notes:
Defining the LSI - 
An Epithelial_dysplastic cell is labelled "LSI" if its nearest Stromal neighbor is within 50µm.
A Stromal cell is labelled "LSI" if its nearest Epithelial_dysplastic neighbor is within 50µm.

Results from SPACEstat - All immune cells distance to the Lesion side

Speaker notes:
to check how far from the LSI dysplastic cells do these outcome group patient's immune cells sit. I checxked for within 50um . 
For the dysplastic side nothing significant here.

Results from SPACEstat - All immune cells distance to the stromal side

Speaker notes:
and from the stromal side, this looks like that quick-reg cases have M1, M2, and DCs sitting closer to the stromal face of the LSI. 
Maybe these myeloid cells (M1, M2, DCs) are patrolling the stromal side, potentially facilitating quick regression from the stromal compartment???

Results from SPACEstat - Immune cells within 50um of LSI

Speaker notes:
Wanting to look closer and zoom in to just the LSI strip and I quantified immune cells 50um within any of the LSI dys or stromal cells.

Results from SPACEstat - Immune cells within 50um of entire LSI

Speaker notes:
Quick regs seem to have more m1 within the LSI region to the rest.

Results from SPACEstat - Immune cells within 50um of DYS side

Results from SPACEstat - Immune cells within 50um of Stromal side

Speaker notes:
M1 and m2 for quick regs enriched on stromal side.

Results from SPACEstat - Immune cells within 50um of Dysplastic epithelium

Speaker notes:
More tregs in the dysplastic epithelium zone and Tregs don't go somewhere for no reason. They go where there's immune activity they need to suppress.
and cd8 is also significant but is definitely outnumbered by tregs so immune suppression.

Results from SPACEstat - Immune cells within 50um of Stroma

Speaker notes:
In quick-reg, the battle is happening mostly in the stroma. The CD8s are working from the stroma, clearing the lesion from the outside in, and apparently that's sufficient. The dysplastic epithelium zone stays relatively quiet immunologically — no Tregs needed there because there's no CD8 threat to suppress there.

Immune cell states - Regressors

Speaker notes:
Checking with functional markers if the immune cell states change for LSI immune cells vs non LSI immune cells.

Immune cell states - Non-Regressors

Results from SPACEstat - Network analysis

Speaker notes:
So for each cell type it connects same-type cells within 15µm into a proximity graph, and any connected component with ≥10 cells becomes a hub.

Results from SPACEstat - Network analysis

Speaker notes:
quick-regressors significantly assemble sparser immune networks — fewer hubs, fewer neighborhoods.

Results from SPACEstat - Network analysis

Speaker notes:
Myeloid cells in quick regs have smaller number of hubs compared to the other groups. We know that they have more myeloids compared to other groups from previous analysis.

Results from SPACEstat - Network analysis

Speaker notes:
This show not only do they have low number of hubs, their myeloid hib sizes are also smaller so the ones present stay dispersed rather than organizing into aggregates.

Results from SPACEstat - Network analysis at LSI

Speaker notes:
Going through our funtional markers, I changed the scale because the difference was not too drastic. After changing we still see the same pattern but most of these markers were poor from what I can remember. 
Coming to area, b cells do have higher area but they have low eccentricity which is higher in macrophages which makes sense. i realised the difference is not too much which i will show next.

Investigating B cell segmentation

B cell mask

Speaker notes:
Looking back at the segmentation, it does not look bad or like many cells are getting combined into one blob to make the area bigger.

Investigating B cell size

Speaker notes:
M1, M2 and Dendritic cells are all 5.6–5.8 µm, smaller than the expected macrophage range of 8–15 µm. This is because Stardist segments nuclei, not whole cells. Macrophage nuclei are actually similar in size to lymphocyte nuclei — it's the cytoplasm that makes macrophages look big under a microscope. So this is expected and correct for nucleus-based segmentation.

Speaker notes:
Neoplastic cells are the largest and most eccentric since dysplastic cells are known to be larger and more irregular shaped

Raw - Enrichment analysis 50um non-reg vs reg & quick-reg vs others

Speaker notes:
After changing the outcome group grouping, this is the enrichment. 50um shows both the obvious RCN that should be enriched in both methods

CLR - Enrichment analysis 50um

Speaker notes:
But after Centered Log-Ratio (CLR) transformation, dysplastic rcn vanishes in count but there is another rcn thats enrtiched but in LDA we still see rcn enriched in dysplatic cells survived So if a patient has 40% Stromal core, the remaining 60% must split among the other 7 RCNs. This means RCN proportions are not independent — when one goes up, others must go down. This creates fake correlations between RCNs that can either hide real differences or create false ones. CLR (Centered Log-Ratio) breaks this constraint by log-transforming and centering each RCN relative to the geometric mean of all RCNs for that patient. After CLR, the values are no longer tied to summing to 1, so each RCN can be tested independently.

Raw - Enrichment analysis 100um(changed comparison)

Speaker notes:
But in 100um, we miss the obvious rcn here

CLR Analysis - Enrichment analysis 100um(changed comparison)

Speaker notes:
but we see a similar pattern compared to 50um count clr so it was good to dig deeper into these 2 RCN’S

Speaker notes:
They were pretty much the same RCN. Its A Normal epithelial-dominated neighborhood (70% normal epithelium + 10% stroma + ~20% immune) is relatively enriched in quick-regressors vs everyone else. The CLR signal shows up at both 50µm and 100µm in Spatial Count, it doesn't show in LDA.
is "more normal epithelium in quick-reg" a biologically interesting claim, or is it just a measurement of how much lesion is present?

Speaker notes:
This ESI is what I would be interested to look into further. on how many immune cells there are, where they are (inside lesion, at the border, far away), and whether they cluster together.

From the preprint, Histolytics identified 3 "themes" of features extracted from each biopsy and Immune Response was one of them.

Speaker notes:
This ESI is what I would be interested to look into further. on how many immune cells there are, where they are (inside lesion, at the border, far away), and whether they cluster together.

More from the pre-print

The other 2 "themes" of features extracted from the biopsies in addition to clinical/HPV information:
Neoplastic Nuclear Morphology — per-nucleus measurements of the abnormal cells: how big they are, how round/irregular, how dark they stain, how clumpy the chromatin is.
Epithelial Structures — the bigger architectural picture: sizes/shapes/proportions of normal squamous tissue, glandular tissue, and neoplastic lesions, plus cell counts in each.

But after cross-validation and final AutoGluon training:

Features ranked best for regressors

Features ranked best for quick-regressors

I also have this top feature values per patient, how should we integrate that?

Some more from the pre-print

What all predicted quick regression:

The high proportion of glandular tissue within the biopsy was the strongest predictor of quick regression- we have glandular cells from epithelial clusters, should we run RCN on these cells? 
The absence of HPV16 was 2nd most important feature in quick regression prediction.

The important immune response features were related to the spatial localization, number and size of immune cell clusters 

High proportions of immune cell clusters close to endocervical glands in the biopsies

RCN Analysis

Spatial count vs LDA

From our image metadata: 
1 pixel = 0.325 micrometers (µm)

Radii we are considering for comparison

100µm = 308px 
150µm = 462px

Grönholm, A. (2025). Profiling of spatial tumor microenvironment architecture in highly multiplexed images of high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (Doctoral dissertation, University of Helsinki). Helda – University of Helsinki. https://helda.helsinki.fi/items/e25ee9ec-c925-4a66-8893-e43cdd5aebc8

Speaker notes:
Spatial count For each cell, draws a circle around it, Counts how many of each cell type are within that circle and then each cell has a "neighbor profile" — e.g., "this cell has 5 tumor neighbors, 2 T-cells, 1 macrophage nearby" LDA Instead of raw neighbor counts, it finds "themes" or "motifs", recurring patterns of cell type combinations Clustering for both Uses K-Means to group cells with similar neighbor profiles. The "elbow method" helps decide how many (clusters) to create

SPATIAL COUNT
K values in the range of 8-9

SPATIAL LDA 
K values in the range of 7-10

Spatial count vs LDA

From our image metadata: 
1 pixel = 0.325 micrometers (µm)

Radii we are considering for comparison
10µm = 31px
30µm = 92px
60µm = 185px
90µm = 277px

Grönholm, A. (2025). Profiling of spatial tumor microenvironment architecture in highly multiplexed images of high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (Doctoral dissertation, University of Helsinki). Helda – University of Helsinki. https://helda.helsinki.fi/items/e25ee9ec-c925-4a66-8893-e43cdd5aebc8

Speaker notes:
Spatial count For each cell, draws a circle around it, Counts how many of each cell type are within that circle and then each cell has a "neighbor profile" — e.g., "this cell has 5 tumor neighbors, 2 T-cells, 1 macrophage nearby" LDA Instead of raw neighbor counts, it finds "themes" or "motifs", recurring patterns of cell type combinations Clustering for both Uses K-Means to group cells with similar neighbor profiles. The "elbow method" helps decide how many (clusters) to create

Elbow curve for optimal K value

Spatial count

Spatial LDA

Speaker notes:
Ran elbow curve to check how many clusters to use so we get distinct neighbourhoods, it was between 8-10 in general, there was slight difference in smaller radii but it stayed consistent in 60 and 90 um.

Comparison : 10 um (k=9)

Speaker notes:
At the smallest radius, spatial count produces mixed clusters that are difficult to interpret because at such a short range, very few cells fall within the neighbourhood window, making the raw counts sparse and noisy. LDA still produces clean, interpretable clusters at this scale because it learns neighbourhood patterns across the entire dataset rather than relying on counts alone.

Comparison : 10 um (k=10)

Comparison : 30 um (k=9)

Speaker notes:
As the radius increases to 30µm, spatial count begins to improve but LDA still has a clear edge in cluster purity.

Comparison : 30 um (k=8)

Speaker notes:
By 60µm, both methods produce similarly interpretable neighbourhoods, suggesting a convergence point where enough cells are captured for spatial count to be reliable

Comparison : 60 um (k=8)

Comparison : 90 um (k=8)

Speaker notes:
At 90µm, an interesting reversal occurs — spatial count produces cleaner clusters while LDA becomes noisier.
